text: Hello all. Welcome to an episode of This is My Architecture. My name is Aisha and with me today
I have Brian joining us from Resmet. Hi Brian. Delighted to be here. Brian, can you tell us a
little bit about Resmet? Absolutely, yeah. Resmet is a med tech company which specializes in sleep
apnea therapy so we build medical devices and digital solutions. Recently you underwent a major
migration where you migrated your on-premises monolithic architecture for your product MyAir
to a completely serverless environment on AWS. Can you talk a little more about that? Yes, MyAir is
our patient engagement platform. So this consists of mobile apps, website, a medical device and our
backend system and this allows our patients to engage and track their therapy. Okay, so you brought
your architecture with us. Let's dive straight into it. Absolutely, yeah. So the first thing
you notice this is a fully serverless solution and that's direction we wanted to go with for
low operations costs. So for any user here using our mobile app, the communication is over GraphQL
and to AppSync. So AppSync and manage GraphQL server. We wanted to take advantage not just of
GraphQL but we also wanted to take advantage of the synchronization infrastructure that comes
out of the box with AppSync. We didn't want to have to rebuild this. Any requests that come in here,
they'll basically go through something called VTL which is velocity translation language.
So this then basically routes it to a particular Lambda. We have multiple Lambdas and then these
Lambdas call into DynamoDB to either write or read and they execute a bunch of business logic.
Okay, this right there seems like a very robust architecture. Can you talk a little bit about how
you scale for this? Yeah, absolutely. Yeah, so these are all managed services which really helps on the
scaling side. So we have about 60 million Lambda invocations every day and at peak load we're
hitting about 4,000 Lambdas, concurrent Lambdas. So it's incredible to see it in operation.
That's definitely very impressive. Can you talk a bit about how does this IoT device right here fit into
this architecture? Absolutely, yeah. So we have our own solution currently within ResMed which handles
IoT. The difference with this project is we now write this data and we publish this data real time
into Kinesis. There's a little bit of transformation. We get this data and then we write this
into DynamoDB via Lambda Poller which does a bunch of statistical analysis.
Okay, so it seems like DynamoDB right here is acting like a centralized repository not just for users
polling in data but also any data that you collect based on their performance from the IoT device. Is that right?
That's exactly correct. Okay, so how does SNS fit into this?
Yeah, so as soon as this data arrives in here we're taking advantage of DB streams
and what we're doing is we have another Lambda here which essentially interrogates the data that's
just been written to understand what is the most important information that we need to tell the user
about how they slept last night or any issues they were having. This then puts this onto an SNS topic
and then this gets sent out to the user either as a push notification or we can send an email off to SES.
Okay, so not only are users tracking and monitoring their health performances but they're also getting
notified in real time based on any anomalies or any important information that they need to know about
their health? Absolutely, yeah. Correct. And was there any performance improvement that you saw with this
kind of a deployment right here? Yeah, so obviously the you know database access is incredible, incredibly fast and
the data that comes through the system from the medical device and so what happens with when someone
wakes up in the morning that's that's when we send up the data to the cloud. Typically in the past it was
taking a few minutes and for this database to arrive and so it was it was measured in minutes and so right
now this data is less than 10 seconds to make it all the way through the system. That's impressive.
And finally I see CloudFront and S3S, so what are these two components doing here in this architecture?
Yeah, so within here we have a lot of static content that we basically stored in here. This static content
is things like videos, images and any other assets which drive the mobile applications. So we support
currently right now we support about 30 countries and 20 languages. So potentially any asset in here
could be represented 600 times based on that matrix. So when the app calls in we have CloudFront in
front of S3 and so we have a lambda at edge here and this lambda at edge interrogates the headers
within that request and determines what language, what country and it has a lookup table which then
basically tells CloudFront here this is the URL within S3 that you need to call and it calls that. And then
this reduces instead of having the 600 potential assets we have about 60 so 10x reduction. So for
maintenance that's very very beneficial. That's very very impressive Brian. So I see that you have
users across 30 countries across the globe so anything that you wanted to talk about the global scale of
this architecture? Yeah well I mean it scales incredibly and a lot of it is out of the box because
it's serverless so we didn't have to do too much tweaking on that and to be able to hit these numbers
it's just amazing. Okay thank you for sharing this with us Brian. No problem at all. Thank you for
watching this is my architecture.
